features.name: ACID Compliance & Transactions
features.description: Guarantees strong transactional consistency and integrity for relational schemas across zonal or global deployments.

features.name: Automated Backups & PITR
features.description: Enables point-in-time recovery (PITR) to restore database states back to a specific millisecond in case of corruption.

features.name: High Availability Failover
features.description: Deploys active-passive synchronous replication across zones, executing automatic failover during outages.

iamRoles.description: Full access to administer Cloud SQL instances, backups, and user credentials.
Full administrative permissions over Cloud Spanner databases, schemas, and queries.

cliExamples.description: Create a high-availability PostgreSQL database instance
List all read replicas attached to database instances

design: Relational databases are chosen when workloads require full ACID transactional guarantees and complex table joins.
Cloud SQL supports standard MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server, scaling vertically through custom machine types.
Cloud Spanner is designed for global relational scalability, offering horizontal writes and transactional consistency.
For Cloud Spanner, primary key design must avoid sequential values (like timestamps) to prevent partition hotspots.
AlloyDB is a PostgreSQL-compatible analytical and transactional database built for high-performance enterprise engines.
Database schema migrations require executing Schema Conversion Tool and Database Migration Service (DMS).
Read replicas offload read-heavy dashboard query traffic from the primary transactional instance.
Use database connection pools to prevent exhausting database thread connection limits.
security: Connect securely to Cloud SQL using the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, which whitelists connections without public IPs.
Database IAM Authentication allows users and service accounts to login using Google credentials instead of static passwords.
Enforce private IP configurations using Serverless VPC Access or Private Service Connect for database interaction.
Always encrypt database backups and transaction logs using Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via KMS.
VPC Service Controls protect databases from data exfiltration by creating a secure network boundary.
Enable SSL/TLS configurations on client connection strings to secure data in transit.
Database administration actions (e.g. changing root password, backups) are audited in Cloud Audit Logs.
Implement row-level and column-level database security to restrict unauthorized service accounts from reading sensitive tables.
reliability: HA configurations use synchronous replication to a standby instance in a different zone in the same region.
Standard read replicas replicate asynchronously, which may lead to replication lag during high write spikes.
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) uses write-ahead logging (WAL) to enable restores to any timestamp within the log retention window.
Perform regular automated daily backups, setting retention policies to match regulatory compliance needs.
Cross-region replicas provide disaster recovery, enabling prompt promote-to-primary actions during region failure.
Cloud Spanner provides an availability SLA of 99.999% for multi-regional instances using Paxos-based replication.
Establish monitoring metrics on database CPU usage, memory usage, and storage growth rate.
Auto-resize storage triggers disk expansion automatically, preventing database crashes due to full disks.
cost: Relational databases charge for provisioned compute (vCPUs/RAM), database storage (GB/month), and backup storage.
Read replicas are billed as full independent database instances, increasing compute cost.
Storage is billed on provisioned capacity, not active database contents; size disks carefully.
Configure storage auto-resize with a safety threshold to prevent runaway disk storage billing.
Use committed use discounts (CUDs) for database instances running continuously in production environments.
Schedule development database shutdowns (e.g., stopping non-HA instances overnight) to minimize off-hour billing.
Review Recommender suggestions to downsize database instances with low historical CPU and memory utilization.
Prune old, unused database snapshots and test backups to minimize storage cost leaks.

features.name: Sub-millisecond Latency
features.description: Optimizes in-memory and NoSQL storage to deliver ultra-low read/write latency at scale.

features.name: Flexible Schema & Documents
features.description: Supports unstructured or semi-structured data formats, allowing rapid indexing without schema migrations.

features.name: Automatic Scaling & Partitioning
features.description: Shards data dynamically across nodes to maintain high performance under heavy read/write volumes.

iamRoles.description: Full control over Bigtable instances, tables, and replication policies.
Full administrative access over Firestore and Datastore databases.

cliExamples.description: Create a Memorystore Redis instance for caching
List all running Bigtable clusters in the project

design: NoSQL databases (Bigtable, Firestore) are chosen for high-throughput, schema-less, or unstructured document storage.
Cloud Bigtable is designed for analytical and high-throughput operational workloads, supporting single-row transactions only.
Bigtable row key design must be lexicographically sorted and designed carefully to avoid database partition hotspots.
Firestore is a document-based database optimized for mobile apps, offering real-time synchronization and automatic indexing.
Memorystore provides fully managed in-memory cache supporting Redis, Valkey, and Memcached engines.
Valkey 9.0 introduces pipeline memory prefetching and zero-copy responses for performance gains.
Choose Redis for complex data types (lists, sorted sets) and Memcached for simple key-value string caching.
Use Firestore in Native mode for mobile/web clients, and Datastore mode for backend server workloads.
security: Secure Memorystore instances by using private services access, isolating caches inside your VPC.
NoSQL databases do not support traditional SQL user privileges; manage access strictly using IAM roles.
Use VPC Service Controls perimeters to protect Firestore and Bigtable data from exfiltration.
Data-at-rest is encrypted using Google default keys, with options to configure KMS keys (CMEK).
Enable Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to protect application endpoints communicating with NoSQL databases.
Audit data-access logs to monitor which service accounts are querying sensitive document databases.
Configure security rules in Firestore Native mode to authenticate mobile client requests directly.
Restrict client access scopes on VM instances connecting to Bigtable API endpoints.
reliability: Bigtable replication allows active-active read/write access across multiple geographical clusters.
Firestore replicates data across multiple zones (regional) or multiple regions (multi-regional) for high availability.
Memorystore Redis High Availability uses primary-replica configurations with automated failover and health checks.
Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster configurations handle automatic promotion of replicas when the primary node fails.
Set appropriate Redis eviction policies (e.g. volatile-lru) to handle cache exhaustion gracefully.
Automatic sharding in Firestore handles high write loads, splitting documents across internal servers.
Configure client-side retry policies with backoff to handle transient NoSQL connection errors.
Back up Firestore databases regularly using automated export tasks saved to Cloud Storage.
cost: Bigtable charges for compute nodes, storage (SSD vs HDD), and network egress between clusters.
Firestore pricing is based on document read, write, and delete counts, not provisioned compute.
Memorystore Redis charges based on cache capacity (GB) and node count (Standard vs Basic tier).
Select Bigtable HDD storage for massive cold analytical data, and SSD for low-latency operational workloads.
Limit unnecessary Firestore read operations by caching query results on client devices.
Configure TTL (Time to Live) policies in Firestore to automatically delete expired documents, reducing storage fees.
Avoid using over-provisioned Memorystore cache sizes; size capacity matching active memory consumption.
Analyze bigquery-to-bigtable integration queries to ensure you are not paying for double transfer.

features.name: Zero Server Management
features.description: Completely abstracts physical servers, VMs, and OS patching, allowing developers to focus solely on code.

features.name: Instant Autoscaling
features.description: Scales compute instances up instantly to handle heavy incoming traffic spikes, and down to zero when idle.

features.name: Event-Driven Invocations
features.description: Triggers code execution automatically in response to events from Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, or HTTP requests.

iamRoles.description: Full administrative control over Cloud Run services, configurations, and revisions.
Full access to create, update, delete, and configure Cloud Functions.

cliExamples.description: Deploy a containerized application to Cloud Run
Deploy an event-driven Cloud Function triggered by a Pub/Sub topic

design: Serverless compute platforms are selected for stateless, variable, or event-driven applications.
Cloud Run runs stateless containers, supporting any binary, framework, or language.
Cloud Run functions (formerly Cloud Functions) deploy small snippets of code for simple, isolated tasks.
App Engine Standard is a PaaS for standard languages, offering fast scaling and pre-configured runtimes.
App Engine Flexible runs custom Docker containers on managed Compute Engine VMs, scaling slower than Standard.
Cloud Run supports up to 250 concurrent requests per container instance, reducing cold start rates.
Serverless VPC Access connectors are required to access resources (like Cloud SQL, Memorystore) with private IPs.
Applications must be stateless; write local scratch data only to the ephemeral /tmp directory.
security: Manage public service availability using IAM roles (allUsers for public, specific users/accounts for private).
Assign a custom Service Account to your serverless service to enforce least privilege to other GCP APIs.
Integrate Cloud Run/App Engine with Cloud Armor by routing traffic through an external HTTP(S) Load Balancer.
Mount secrets from Secret Manager directly as environment variables or volume mounts instead of hardcoding.
Configure Ingress settings to 'Internal' to prevent external traffic from accessing internal microservices.
Restrict serverless functions from accessing other projects using project-specific VPC Service Controls.
Verify container image security using Artifact Analysis before deploying them to Cloud Run.
Enable Binary Authorization to block deployment of unsigned, untrusted container images.
reliability: Serverless runtimes automatically replicate application containers across multiple zones in the region.
Set the minimum instances configuration to '1' or more to eliminate cold-start latency for key endpoints.
Autoscaling limits (maximum instances) prevent runaway scaling and protect downstream databases from crashing.
Configure HTTP health checks to verify that container instances are ready before routing traffic to them.
Canary deployments are supported out of the box using traffic splitting between revision states.
Logs written to stdout/stderr are captured automatically and exported directly to Cloud Logging.
Event-driven functions process events asynchronously, supporting automatic retries on execution failures.
Design services to handle duplicate events gracefully, maintaining idempotency.
cost: Serverless platforms bill to the nearest 100ms based on CPU and memory allocated during active request execution.
Idle resources cost zero because instances auto-scale down to zero when request traffic stops.
Choose CPU allocation mode: 'CPU allocated only during request processing' (saves cost) vs 'CPU always allocated'.
Set max instances to cap maximum possible spending and protect against DDoS billing spikes.
Optimize container startup times (e.g. lightweight base images) to reduce billing during scaling cold starts.
Tune container request concurrency limits to maximize resource efficiency per active instance.
Use Cloud Billing budgets and Pub/Sub notifications to automate service shutdown if cost thresholds are crossed.
Take advantage of the generous free tiers offered monthly for Cloud Run and Cloud Run functions.

features.name: Full OS Control
features.description: Provides root-level administrative access to configure, patch, and run custom operating systems and legacy applications.

features.name: Custom Hardware Allocation
features.description: Allows tailoring vCPUs, RAM, persistent disks, and GPUs to match precise application workload profiles.

features.name: Preemptible & Spot Discounts
features.description: Supports using short-lived instances for batch workloads, offering up to 91% pricing discounts.

iamRoles.description: Full access to administer all Compute Engine VM instances, disks, networks, and configurations.
Permissions to create, modify, start, stop, and delete virtual machine instances.

cliExamples.description: Create a virtual machine instance in a specific subnet
Attach a zonal persistent disk to an existing VM

design: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (Compute Engine) is chosen for legacy applications requiring root OS control.
Custom machine types allow specifying precise numbers of vCPUs and GBs of memory to prevent over-allocation.
Sole-tenant nodes run virtual machines on dedicated physical hardware for compliance and license requirements.
Shielded VMs protect virtual instances against boot-level rootkits using vTPM and secure boot checks.
Local SSDs offer high IOPS and low latency, but data is lost when the instance is stopped or terminated.
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) automate the scaling, repair, and deployment of identical virtual machines.
Startup and shutdown scripts automate software installs and cleanup processes on instance boots.
Use VM templates to maintain standard golden machine configurations across the enterprise.
security: Do not use default Service Accounts with editor roles; assign custom service accounts with minimal roles.
Manage VM access using OS Login, which binds SSH keys directly to users' GCP IAM credentials.
Block all external SSH access by using Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) SSH tunneling.
Firewall rules are stateful and applied to VMs using target network tags.
Configure Private Google Access on subnets to allow VMs without public IPs to connect to Google APIs.
Enable UEFI Secure Boot on Shielded VMs to validate operating system kernel signatures.
Encrypt VM disks using Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) managed via Cloud KMS.
Audit operating system access and console administrative changes using Cloud Audit Logs.
reliability: Live Migration automatically moves running instances to another host during Google host maintenance.
Regional Managed Instance Groups distribute instances across zones, protecting against single-zone failures.
Configure MIG Autohealing using HTTP health checks to automatically recreate crashed VMs.
Schedule regular persistent disk snapshots, storing copies in Cloud Storage for disaster recovery.
Spot VMs have no SLA and are subject to immediate reclaim with a 30-second preemption notice.
Configure VM shutdown scripts to run for up to 90 seconds, saving state before termination.
Zonal persistent disks are replicated across physical drives in a zone; regional disks replicate across two zones.
Utilize regional load balancers to route traffic to healthy virtual machines in different zones.
cost: Compute Engine charges for virtual machine vCPU/RAM allocation, persistent disk size, and network egress.
Resource-based Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) offer deep savings for predictable 1 or 3-year commitments.
Spot/Preemptible VMs offer up to 91% discount, ideal for batch, testing, or stateless workloads.
Use MIG Autoscaling to scale down instance counts during off-peak hours, saving compute costs.
Identify and delete orphaned persistent disks and unassigned static external IP addresses.
Use Recommender insights to downsize VM machine types with low historical CPU and memory utilization.
Stop non-production development VMs automatically overnight using Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions.
Local SSDs are billed based on capacity; delete them when compute tasks are complete.

features.name: Serverless Data Warehouse
features.description: Executes SQL queries across petabytes of structured data without managing servers, indexes, or clusters.

features.name: Separated Storage & Compute
features.description: Decouples query processing slots from physical data storage, optimizing performance and budget allocations.

features.name: Real-Time Machine Learning
features.description: Enables training and running machine learning models directly inside the data warehouse using standard SQL.

iamRoles.description: Full access to BigQuery resources, datasets, tables, queries, and security settings.
Read-only access to datasets, tables, views, and execution results.

cliExamples.description: Load a CSV file from Cloud Storage into a BigQuery table
Run a SQL query and save output to a destination table
cliExamples.command:
bq query --destination_table=my-dataset.results-table \
  --use_legacy_sql=false \
  'SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) FROM `my-project.my-dataset.sales` GROUP BY user_id'

design: BigQuery is a serverless, highly scalable cloud data warehouse for structured and semi-structured data.
Decoupled architecture scales storage and compute slots independently based on active queries.
Partitioning tables (by ingestion time, date/timestamp, or integer range) limits data scanned during queries.
Clustering tables orders data based on column values, working with partitioning to speed up query execution.
BigQuery Omni allows running queries across multi-cloud environments (AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage).
Dataproc runs managed Hadoop and Spark clusters, migrating legacy on-premises big data systems to GCP.
BigQuery BI Engine caches query states in memory to accelerate dashboard and report rendering.
Use materialized views to cache the results of complex queries and speed up downstream analytics.
security: Authorize views to share query results with users without giving them access to the underlying tables.
Enforce row-level security and column-level security using policy tags to restrict access to sensitive fields.
Manage BigQuery access using IAM dataset roles (`roles/bigquery.dataViewer`, `roles/bigquery.admin`).
Encrypt analytical datasets at rest using Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS.
VPC Service Controls perimeters protect analytical databases from data exfiltration and public queries.
Audit data-access logs to monitor which service accounts and users are running queries on datasets.
Mask sensitive PII fields inside table columns automatically using Sensitive Data Protection integrations.
Secure connection access by disabling public datasets whitelisting on internal project datasets.
reliability: BigQuery replicates datasets across multiple physical data centers in the selected region.
Datasets can be configured as multi-regional (e.g. US or EU) for geographical redundancy and failover.
Time travel enables recovering deleted or modified table states up to 7 days in the past.
Dataproc clusters can be configured as high availability, deploying three master nodes to prevent control plane failures.
Write queries using standard SQL with built-in query optimization and syntax verification.
System metrics, query execution logs, and load jobs are logged automatically to Cloud Logging.
Failed query jobs due to transient errors support automated client retries with backoff.
Export datasets to Cloud Storage regularly to create offline historical database backups.
cost: BigQuery storage is billed per GB/month; active storage transitions to long-term storage (50% discount) if unmodified for 90 days.
Query billing has two models: On-demand (billed per TB of data scanned) and Capacity (flat-rate billing using slots).
Prune scanned data and reduce costs by always including partitioning columns in query WHERE clauses.
Avoid using SELECT *; always query only the specific columns needed to minimize TB scan size.
Use slots reservations to allocate query budgets across development, staging, and production teams.
Set maximum query billing limits in the console to block run-away queries from running.
Clean up temporary tables and query outputs by configuring table expiration policies.
Review cost optimization recommendation logs to identify infrequently queried tables to archive.

features.name: Serverless Build Execution
features.description: Compiles, tests, and packages code in isolated, serverless container sandboxes that scale dynamically.

features.name: Progressive Delivery Control
features.description: Orchestrates multi-stage application releases to GKE, Cloud Run, and Anthos with automated canary checks.

features.name: Vulnerability Scanning
features.description: Scans container images and language packages automatically for CVEs during the build and storage stages.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, run, and cancel Cloud Build pipelines and triggers.
Permissions to manage continuous delivery releases, targets, and rollouts.

cliExamples.description: Trigger a Cloud Build execution using a local config file
Create a release in Cloud Deploy for a specific pipeline

design: DevOps pipelines automate the compilation, packaging, testing, and deployment of software artifacts.
Cloud Build executes declarative build steps defined in YAML or JSON, running in isolated container environments.
Cloud Deploy manages continuous delivery pipelines, automating progressive rollouts to GKE and Cloud Run.
Artifact Registry stores and governs container images, Helm charts, and Maven/npm packages in a single registry.
Developer Connect establishes secure Git proxies between GCP services and external code repositories.
Secure Source Manager hosts private Git repositories with enterprise compliance, CMEK, and IAM.
Use Cloud Build triggers to automate build runs on Git commits, pull requests, or branch updates.
Deploy pipelines must support immutable tags; do not use the 'latest' tag in production releases.
security: Enforce Binary Authorization policies in target environments to block containers that lack build pipeline signatures.
Store API keys, SSH keys, and passwords in Secret Manager and fetch them dynamically during the build run.
Segregate IAM duties: developers write code, build accounts compile, release roles approve production deployments.
Vulnerability scanning in Artifact Registry automatically flags security threats inside dependencies.
Run build workers inside a private pool VPC network to connect to databases and internal services safely.
Encrypt code repositories and stored packages in Artifact Registry using Customer-Managed Keys (CMEK).
Restrict build agent Service Accounts to least privilege, avoiding owner or editor roles.
All build outputs, compiler logs, and deployment approvals are logged permanently in Cloud Audit Logs.
reliability: Cloud Build executes builds in serverless sandboxes, scaling automatically to handle parallel jobs.
Cloud Deploy supports automated rollback to a previous successful release when rollout health checks fail.
Artifact Registry replicates stored packages across multiple zones or regions to ensure high availability.
Store pipeline configs and build manifests in Git to enable recovery via GitOps practices.
Cache intermediate compiler dependencies in Cloud Storage to prevent build crashes due to external package network failures.
Monitor rollout statuses, deployment speeds, and build error rates in Cloud Monitoring dashboards.
Configure pipeline triggers to run tests and linters, preventing broken code from reaching production.
Maintain immutable build logs and release archives to guarantee traceability.
cost: Cloud Build charges based on machine type (virtual machine shape) and execution duration (per minute).
Artifact Registry bills for package storage capacity (GB/month) and network transfer egress.
Cloud Deploy charges based on active delivery pipelines and target deployments executed.
Enable caching of build steps to speed up pipelines and minimize active build billing minutes.
Establish cleanup policies in Artifact Registry to delete old, noncurrent, or untagged container images.
Use standard, smaller build worker machines unless compilation tasks explicitly require high CPU/RAM.
Set build timeout limits in pipeline files to terminate stalled builds and prevent runaway billing.
Take advantage of monthly free tiers (e.g. 120 build-minutes free per day on standard machines).

features.name: Global VPC Topologies
features.description: Constructs virtual network topologies globally across GCP, organizing regional subnets in a private boundary.

features.name: High-Speed Interconnect
features.description: Connects on-premises datacenters directly to GCP networks via Dedicated or Partner fiber lines (up to 100 Gbps).

features.name: Dynamic BGP Routing
features.description: Exchanges route tables dynamically between Cloud Routers and on-premises networks using dynamic routing.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, modify, and delete networking resources, VPCs, subnets, and firewalls.
Read-only access to VPC topologies, routing configurations, and IP allocations.

cliExamples.description: Create a custom Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network
Create a regional subnet inside a VPC network
cliExamples.command:
gcloud compute networks subnets create my-subnet \
  --network=my-custom-vpc \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --range=10.10.1.0/24 \
  --enable-private-ip-google-access

design: VPC networks are global resources; subnets are regional resources hosting virtual machines and network interfaces.
Shared VPC allows a host project to share subnets with service projects, centralizing network control.
VPC Network Peering connects two VPCs securely with low latency; routing is non-transitive.
Cloud Router uses Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) to exchange routes dynamically between VPCs and external systems.
HA VPN deploys two active tunnels with dynamic routing to guarantee a 99.99% connection uptime SLA.
Cloud Interconnect provides physical fiber connections; Dedicated connects directly, Partner connects via providers.
VPC network peering requires that IP CIDR blocks do not overlap between the peered networks.
Configure Private Google Access on subnets to allow VMs with only internal IPs to access Google APIs.
security: Firewall rules are stateful and applied to VM instances using network tags, service accounts, or IP ranges.
Hierarchical firewall policies apply network rules at the Organization or Folder levels, enforcing corporate standards.
Cloud NAT allows private VMs to access the internet for updates while blocking incoming connections.
Audit network connections, blocked ports, and packets using VPC Flow Logs.
Enable Firewall Rules Logging to audit which rules are actively blocking or allowing traffic.
Secure hybrid connections by encrypting Cloud Interconnect traffic using MACsec or IPsec VPN tunnels.
Use Private Service Connect to expose internal services to other VPCs privately without IP peering.
Restrict administrative network changes using the networkAdmin IAM role.
reliability: HA VPN requires deploying two tunnels from different gateway interfaces to distinct public peer IPs.
Cloud Router automatically handles path failover by dynamically updating routing paths via BGP.
Dedicated Interconnect offers 99.99% availability when deployed with dual circuits across two edge locations.
Replicate subnets across multiple zones in a region to ensure high availability for compute nodes.
Configure health checks on load balancers to route traffic away from unhealthy network zones.
VPC networks support automatic subnet routing, which propagates routes globally.
Monitor network latency and packet drops using Network Intelligence Center.
Dynamic routing mode must be set to 'Global' to allow routers to propagate routes across all regions.
cost: VPC networks do not charge for network creation; billing is based on network data transfer (egress).
Data transfer within the same zone is free, but cross-zone transfer within the region incurs network fees.
Cross-region data egress is billed based on source and destination region rates; design zones carefully.
Cloud NAT charges per gateway hour and for data processed; do not deploy NAT gateways in idle subnets.
Dedicated Interconnect reduces overall cross-region egress billing compared to standard internet egress.
Prune unused static external IP addresses to avoid hourly idle reservation fees.
VPC Flow Logs generate large volumes of metadata; configure sampling rates to control logging costs.
Use Shared VPC to consolidate network infrastructure, reducing the number of VPN gateways and NATs needed.

features.name: Global HTTP(S) Load Balancing
features.description: Acts as a single global IP address entry point, routing requests to the nearest healthy backend region.

features.name: Cloud CDN Edge Caching
features.description: Caches static web assets at Google's global Edge Points of Presence (PoPs) to minimize latency.

features.name: Globally Redundant DNS
features.description: Translates domain names to IP addresses globally using Google's redundant anycast nameserver network.

iamRoles.description: Full access to configure load balancers, SSL certificates, and URL maps.
Permissions to create, modify, and delete DNS zones, records, and policies.

cliExamples.description: Create a global static external IP address for a load balancer
Create a DNS managed zone for domain routing
cliExamples.command:
gcloud dns managed-zones create my-zone \
  --dns-name="example.com." \
  --description="My domain zone"

design: Cloud Load Balancing distributes traffic across multiple virtual machines, containers, or serverless backends.
Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer is a Layer 7 proxy using a single Anycast IP, supporting URL path routing.
SSL Proxy and TCP Proxy are global Layer 4 load balancers for non-HTTP traffic, terminating SSL at edge.
Network Load Balancer (NLB) is a regional, non-proxy Layer 4 load balancer that preserves client IP addresses.
Cloud CDN caches static content close to users at Google edge points, integrating with HTTP(S) load balancers.
Cloud DNS provides highly scalable, low-latency domain name resolution using anycast routing.
Configure DNS routing policies (geo-routing, failover) to manage global traffic destination paths.
Use URL maps to define routing rules, sending traffic to specific backend buckets or VM groups.
security: Integrate Cloud Armor with HTTP(S) Load Balancers to defend against Layer 7 OWASP Top 10 and DDoS attacks.
Terminate SSL/TLS connections at the load balancer using Google-managed SSL certificates with automatic renewal.
Access control to backend resources can be restricted by enabling Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) on the load balancer.
Enable DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) in Cloud DNS to prevent cache poisoning and spoofing.
Configure SSL policies on the load balancer to restrict weak ciphers and enforce TLS version limits.
Protect backend servers by placing them in private subnets, allowing traffic only from the load balancer proxy IPs.
Use backend service signing keys to secure CDN cache configurations (signed URLs/cookies).
Enable HTTPS-only redirection at the load balancer level to enforce transport encryption.
reliability: Global load balancers automatically route traffic around regional outages to healthy backend regions.
Configure multi-region backend services with capacity limits, letting the load balancer spill over traffic automatically.
Managed health checks verify VM/container readiness, immediately draining connections from failed nodes.
Cloud DNS has an availability SLA of 100% supported by Google's global nameserver network.
Configure DNS failover policies to route users to backup cloud resources during primary application failures.
Cloud CDN uses automatic cache bypass during backend failures to ensure dynamic content delivery.
Load balancers handle sudden traffic spikes gracefully without requiring warming.
Use session affinity settings to bind client requests to the same backend node when state is required.
cost: Load balancing charges are based on forwarding rules, ingress data processed (GB), and backend connections.
Cloud CDN bills for cache egress network fees, cache fill operations, and cache lookup requests.
Cloud DNS charges per managed zone and for millions of DNS queries processed per month.
Cloud CDN significantly reduces network egress billing by serving cached files from edge points instead of backend VMs.
Use Google-managed SSL certificates to eliminate the purchase and manual renewal cost of certificates.
Consolidate multiple domains under a single global load balancer using URL host rules to minimize rule charges.
Configure CDN cache TTLs (Time to Live) to maximize cache hit ratios, reducing backend compute costs.
Delete unused forwarding rules and static IP reservations to avoid idle network fees.

features.name: Web Application Firewall (WAF)
features.description: Filters Layer 7 traffic to block SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and malicious bots at the edge.

features.name: VPC Service Controls
features.description: Creates secure cryptographic perimeters around GCP APIs, blocking unauthorized data movement and access.

features.name: Source IP Whitelisting
features.description: Enforces strict source IP whitelisting and geo-blocking policies on public endpoints.

iamRoles.description: Full access to configure security policies, firewalls, and Cloud Armor rules.
Permissions to manage VPC Service Controls perimeters and access levels.

cliExamples.description: Create a Cloud Armor security policy
Add a rate limiting rule to a Cloud Armor policy

design: Perimeter security services block threat vectors before they reach your internal application hosts.
Google Cloud Armor provides DDoS defense and WAF rules, filtering traffic at the edge of the load balancer.
VPC Service Controls (VPC SC) isolate Google APIs, preventing data exfiltration outside the project.
Access Context Manager defines access levels based on device state, user identity, and source IP.
Secure remote connections using Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to enforce zero-trust access control.
Hierarchical firewall policies allow organization admins to apply firewall rules across all child projects.
Private Google Access allows instances on private subnets to connect to APIs inside the VPC SC perimeter.
Use Cloud NAT to translate private IPs to public IPs for outbound updates without allowing inbound connections.
security: VPC Service Controls prevent copying data from a secure project bucket to an external public bucket.
Cloud Armor preconfigured rules protect against the OWASP Top 10 web vulnerabilities (e.g. SQLi, XSS).
Enable rate limiting in Cloud Armor to block brute-force login attempts and scraping bots.
IAP checks credentials and device policies (using context-aware access) before granting web access.
Secure SSH/RDP ports on VM instances by removing public IPs and routing through IAP tunnels.
Use bridge perimeters in VPC SC to allow secure, explicit database communication between separate perimeters.
Cloud Armor Threat Intelligence blocks requests originating from known malicious IP lists (tor exit nodes, bots).
Regularly review security violations in the VPC SC dry-run mode to audit perimeter health.
reliability: Cloud Armor is deployed across Google's edge locations, absorbing massive volumetric DDoS attacks.
VPC Service Controls configurations are replicated globally, ensuring authorization checks do not fail during local outages.
Access Context Manager policies are highly available and evaluated in real-time at the edge.
VPC SC dry-run mode allows testing perimeters without blocking active application traffic.
Configure Cloud Armor to redirect blocked traffic to a honeypot or custom warning page.
Enforce fail-close policies on security perimeters to prioritize data safety during misconfigurations.
System alerts, security blocks, and perimeter attempts are logged in real-time to Cloud Logging.
Establish redundant IAP access pathways (e.g., bastion host backup) to prevent operator lockout.
cost: Cloud Armor charges per security policy, per rule, and per million queries evaluated.
VPC Service Controls and Access Context Manager are core platform services offered at no charge.
Identify and optimize Cloud Armor rule complexity, as evaluating complex custom regex rules is billed higher.
Enable Cloud Armor Standard tier for basic application defense; upgrade to Cloud Armor Enterprise for high-traffic environments.
Dry-run configurations in VPC SC help avoid costly production downtime from blocked APIs during setups.
Reduce Cloud Armor costs by filtering simple traffic at the firewall level before edge evaluation.
Analyze Cloud Armor logs using Cloud Logging sink filters to minimize Log storage billing.
Consolidate security perimeters across multiple projects to reduce management overhead and complexity costs.

features.name: Resource Hierarchy Governance
features.description: Enforces resource hierarchy access policies from Organization to Project, inheriting permissions downwards.

features.name: Least Privilege Role Controls
features.description: Assigns fine-grained, predefined roles to user groups, preventing over-privileged credentials.

features.name: Zero-Trust Remote Access
features.description: Secures web applications and administrative consoles without VPNs using Identity-Aware Proxy.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, modify, and delete IAM policies across resources.
Permissions to create and manage projects within folders or organizations.

cliExamples.description: Grant a service account the Storage Object Viewer role
List all IAM role bindings configured on a project

design: Identity and Access Management (IAM) defines who (identity) has what access (role) on which resource.
Resource hierarchy (Organization -> Folders -> Projects -> Resources) enforces inherited policy controls.
Permissions inherit downwards; policy restrictions cannot deny permissions granted at higher resource levels.
Assign roles to Google Groups instead of individual users to simplify access administration.
Service Accounts are identities for applications; they do not have passwords and use public/private key pairs.
User-managed service account keys are a security risk; rotate them regularly or use Workload Identity.
IAM Conditions grant access to resources only when conditions match (e.g., specific date, IP, resource naming).
Directory Sync syncs identities from external providers (Active Directory, Azure AD) into Cloud Identity.
security: Enforce least privilege by avoiding primitive roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) in production environments.
Enable Service Account Impersonation, letting developers generate short-lived credentials instead of downloading JSON keys.
Workload Identity Federation allows external workloads (AWS, Azure, GitHub Actions) to authenticate without static keys.
Use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to enforce access controls on administrative web apps.
Audit administrative access and role assignments by regularly scanning Cloud Audit Logs.
Enable Organization Policies to block the creation of external service account keys entirely.
Use IAM Recommender to identify over-privileged users and automatically strip unused permissions.
Configure Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) in Cloud Identity for all enterprise users.
reliability: IAM authorization checks are globally distributed and cached at edge points, ensuring sub-millisecond evaluation.
Identity metadata is replicated globally across Google Cloud datacenters for high availability.
If external identity syncing fails, cached credentials in Cloud Identity allow continued operation.
Design critical service accounts with backup permissions to ensure automation pipelines do not fail.
SSO (Single Sign-On) configuration supports automated failover across redundant identity endpoints.
IAM modifications propagate globally within seconds, minimizing access delay.
System authentication errors and access denials are logged to Cloud Logging automatically.
Maintain IAM policy configurations as Code (IaC) in git to enable quick restore from policy errors.
cost: IAM, Cloud Identity Free, and basic Resource Manager services are provided at no cost.
Syncing external identities and using basic IAM authorization check APIs is free.
Audit log storage is billed based on retention; route logs to Cloud Storage Archive to save cost.
Use IAM Recommender suggestions to downsize roles, reducing operational risk and management audit fees.
Consolidate multiple projects into shared folders to simplify policy management, reducing admin overhead.
Automate the identification and deletion of inactive service accounts to save audit processing costs.
Cloud Identity Premium is billed per user; allocate premium licenses only to users requiring advanced device controls.
Minimize cross-project service account access requests to reduce audit log size and related storage billing.

features.name: Centralized Vault Storage
features.description: Provides secure, centralized storage and rotation for passwords, API keys, and certificates.

features.name: Hardware Security Modules (HSM)
features.description: Generates and stores cryptographic keys inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated Hardware Security Modules.

features.name: Automated Key Rotation
features.description: Rotates cryptographic keys and secret versions automatically based on defined schedules.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, modify, and rotate cryptographic keys and key rings.
Permissions to create, modify, access, and rotate secrets inside Secret Manager.

cliExamples.description: Create a new secret in Secret Manager
Add a new secret version with static text
cliExamples.command: echo -n "super-secret-password" | gcloud secrets versions add db-password --data-file=-

design: Secret Manager is chosen to store API keys, database credentials, and certificates securely.
Cloud Key Management Service (KMS) manages cryptographic keys used to encrypt data.
Envelope encryption: Cloud KMS manages Key Encryption Keys (KEK) that encrypt Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) which encrypt actual data.
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) give you control over key rotation and access policies in GCP services.
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) are keys generated on-premises and provided to GCP during API calls.
KMS HSM keys generate and store cryptographic keys inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated physical modules.
Cloud External Key Manager (EKM) stores cryptographic keys outside of Google's network for complete sovereignty.
Secrets and keys are global or regional resources; choose regional storage to comply with data sovereignty regulations.
security: Do not store plaintext passwords in source repositories or container images; pull them from Secret Manager.
Manage access to secrets using roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor, avoiding administrative roles.
KMS key ring permissions must be configured at the key ring or key level, not the project level (least privilege).
Enable automated rotation policies for KMS keys and secrets to mitigate credential compromise risk.
Secret Manager allows configuring VPC Service Controls perimeters to prevent unauthorized secret fetching.
Audit logs track key rotations, key access events, and secret version retrievals.
Disable or destroy compromised KMS key versions immediately to prevent further decryption.
Always encrypt database connection passwords when mounting them to serverless containers.
reliability: KMS and Secret Manager replicate keys and secrets across multiple zones or regions.
Choose automatic replication for Secret Manager to distribute secrets globally for high availability.
Destroyed KMS keys have a 24-hour grace period during which they can be recovered before permanent deletion.
KMS key rings cannot be deleted, preventing accidental loss of keys encrypting legacy archives.
Secret versions are immutable; updates create a new version, allowing safe rollbacks.
KMS API endpoints are highly optimized to ensure decryption calls do not introduce app latency.
System alerts on key expiration or decryption errors are sent to Cloud Monitoring.
Configure backup decryption keys in recovery plans for disaster recovery.
cost: KMS charges per active key version per month and for cryptographic operations performed.
Secret Manager bills per active secret, per secret version, and for API access operations.
Disable or destroy unused KMS key versions to avoid monthly key storage fees.
Secret Manager charges for API operations; cache retrieved secrets in application memory to minimize API billing.
Consolidate keys on shared Key Rings where possible to simplify billing structures.
Avoid using EKM (External Key Manager) for high-frequency operations due to network egress costs.
Take advantage of free tier limits for secret lookups and KMS decryption operations.
Monitor decryption API call rates using billing dashboards to detect inefficient application code.

features.name: Unified Dashboarding
features.description: Consolidates logs, CPU/memory performance metrics, and application traces in real-time dashboards.

features.name: Dynamic Alerting Rules
features.description: Triggers email, Slack, or PagerDuty alerts automatically when metric thresholds are breached.

features.name: Distributed Trace Analysis
features.description: Tracks request execution paths across multiple microservices to pinpoint latency bottlenecks.

iamRoles.description: Full access to configure monitoring dashboards, alerting policies, and uptime checks.
Permissions to configure log sinks, buckets, retention policies, and view all logs.

cliExamples.description: Create a log routing sink to export logs to a Cloud Storage bucket
cliExamples.command:
gcloud logging sinks create my-storage-sink \
  storage.googleapis.com/my-log-bucket \
  --log-filter="severity>=ERROR"

cliExamples.description: List all active alerting policies in the project

design: Google Cloud Observability (formerly Stackdriver) provides logging, monitoring, tracing, and profiling.
Cloud Monitoring collects performance metrics from GCP services, VMs, and custom applications.
Cloud Logging ingests, filters, and routes log lines from applications and audit trails.
Log Sinks route filtered logs to Cloud Storage (archival), BigQuery (analytics), or Pub/Sub (real-time stream).
Cloud Trace analyses request latency across microservices, identifying performance bottlenecks.
Cloud Profiler continuously analyzes CPU and memory allocation patterns inside application code.
Uptime checks monitor external endpoints, alerting operators on availability failures.
Use MQL (Monitoring Query Language) to write advanced metric aggregation and filtering queries.
security: Administrative changes and data access logs are recorded in immutable Admin Activity and Data Access Audit Logs.
Exclude sensitive PII or credentials from logs using Logging exclusions or Sensitive Data Protection.
Manage log access using IAM logging roles (`roles/logging.privateViewer` is required to view audit logs).
Encrypt log buckets at rest using Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS.
Access to Cloud Monitoring dashboards can be restricted to security and operations teams.
Log sinks can export logs to a secure, isolated audit project bucket for compliance.
VPC Service Controls perimeters protect metrics and trace data from external exfiltration.
Set strict log bucket retention periods to comply with data privacy regulations (e.g. GDPR).
reliability: Cloud Logging is serverless, scaling instantly to absorb massive spikes in system logs.
Log routing sinks support automated retries to ensure log delivery to destinations (Storage, BigQuery).
Configure alert notifications to multiple channels (PagerDuty, SMS, Slack, Webhooks) to ensure response.
Uptime checks run from multiple geographical locations worldwide to prevent false positives.
Trace and Profiler agents run in the background with low overhead, ensuring application stability.
Replicate log buckets across regions to secure logs from regional disasters.
Store historical logs in Cloud Storage buckets with Object Lifecycle Lock for WORM compliance.
Create custom metric alerts to detect anomalies in log ingestion rates.
cost: Cloud Logging charges per GB of logs ingested and for logs retained beyond the default 30 days.
Cloud Monitoring charges based on custom metrics ingested and API read/write operations.
Reduce logging costs by setting up Log Exclusions to drop high-volume, low-value debug logs.
Configure log sinks to only route warnings and errors to BigQuery, keeping debug logs in standard storage.
Shorten log bucket retention periods to the minimum required to avoid monthly log storage fees.
Monitor custom metric ingestion rates; avoid exporting high-cardinality metrics (like user IDs) to Monitoring.
Standard GCP service metrics are ingested at no cost; only custom or Prometheus metrics incur fees.
Use Cloud Storage Archive class for long-term log storage to minimize GB/month fees.

features.name: State Machine Execution
features.description: Orchestrates multi-step API and HTTP integrations in reliable serverless state machines with automated retry steps.

features.name: Asynchronous Rate Control
features.description: Buffers execution requests inside task queues, managing retry rates and rate limits to protect backend servers.

features.name: Serverless Cron Scheduler
features.description: Triggers recurring API calls, Pub/Sub events, or workflows on standard crontab schedules.

iamRoles.description: Full administrative control to create, update, and trigger workflows.
Permissions to manage task queues, task creation, and execution settings.

cliExamples.description: Create a Cloud Tasks queue with custom rate limits
Trigger a serverless workflow execution with JSON arguments

design: Orchestration tools connect disparate services in structured, automated, and reliable sequences.
Workflows runs serverless state machines that execute HTTP/API calls, handling errors and states.
Cloud Tasks manages asynchronous execution queues, providing rate control and request buffering.
Cloud Scheduler is a managed cron service that triggers recurring Pub/Sub events or HTTP calls.
Eventarc routes system events (from Cloud Audit Logs, Pub/Sub) to serverless targets (Cloud Run).
Choose Workflows for complex API orchestration and Cloud Tasks for target-rate backpressure queue management.
Cloud Tasks supports both HTTP target tasks and App Engine tasks.
State configurations in Workflows are defined declaratively in YAML or JSON.
security: Assign custom Service Accounts to Workflows to grant permissions to trigger target APIs.
Secure Cloud Tasks workers by using OIDC/OAuth2 tokens in task headers for authentication.
Secure Cloud Scheduler HTTP endpoints by configuring OIDC tokens on trigger requests.
Eventarc triggers enforce IAM roles (roles/eventarc.eventReceiver) on receiving runtimes.
VPC Service Controls protect event routes, task queues, and workflows from unauthorized projects.
Log workflow executions, task completions, and event deliveries to Cloud Logging.
Secrets used during workflow steps must be fetched from Secret Manager at runtime.
Restrict administrative access to queues and schedulers using IAM roles.
reliability: Workflows handles execution state and failures, retrying failed HTTP calls with custom backoff.
Cloud Tasks automatically retries failed tasks based on queue configurations (max double backoff).
Cloud Scheduler guarantees execution, retrying failed cron triggers.
Eventarc is built on Pub/Sub, guaranteeing event delivery to endpoints.
Workflows executions are serverless and highly available, persisting state across failures.
Set maximum concurrent tasks in Cloud Tasks to prevent overloading backend database systems.
Establish dead-letter queues in Eventarc/Pub/Sub to isolate events that fail to deliver.
Monitor task queue lengths and workflow execution latencies in Cloud Monitoring.
cost: Workflows charges based on the number of steps executed during execution runs.
Cloud Tasks bills for operations performed (task creation, execution attempts).
Cloud Scheduler charges per active job per month (up to 3 jobs are free per account).
Eventarc pricing is based on the volume of events routed to destination runtimes.
Reduce Cloud Tasks billing by batching multiple events into a single task payload where possible.
Configure task retry limits to prevent infinite loop executions from accumulating billing fees.
Choose Workflows over compute instances for orchestration to eliminate 24/7 VM hosting costs.
Utilize free tiers available for Workflows steps and Eventarc data volumes monthly.

features.name: Serverless Message Ingestion
features.description: Ingests millions of streaming messages per second with global anycast routing and low-latency storage.

features.name: Unified Stream/Batch Processing
features.description: Executes data transformations across streaming or batch sources using Apache Beam pipelines.

features.name: Data Lake Governance
features.description: Centralizes metadata cataloging, data lineage tracking, and compliance scanning across storage resources.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, publish, and subscribe to Pub/Sub topics and subscriptions.
Permissions to execute, monitor, and cancel Dataflow pipeline jobs.

cliExamples.description: Create a Pub/Sub topic with schema validation
Create a Pub/Sub pull subscription with a 7-day retention period

design: Data pipelines ingest, transform, and load massive data streams for real-time analytics.
Pub/Sub is a serverless, global messaging queue, routing messages from publishers to subscribers.
Dataflow runs managed Apache Beam pipelines for unified stream and batch data processing.
Dataplex manages logical data lakes, consolidating metadata and enforcing governance across storage.
Pub/Sub Lite offers lower cost partitions for predictable, high-volume zonal streaming.
Configure dead-letter topics to route unparseable stream messages for debug analysis.
Dataflow templates allow running standard data transformations without compiling code.
Use Pub/Sub push subscriptions to invoke serverless runtimes directly on message arrivals.
security: Manage publisher and subscriber access separately using specific IAM roles (`roles/pubsub.publisher`).
VPC Service Controls perimeters protect data pipelines from exfiltrating data to external topics.
Data in transit and at rest is encrypted, supporting customer-managed keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS.
Restrict database ingress by executing Dataflow workers on internal subnets with private IPs.
Enable Pub/Sub message schemas to validate message structures before ingestion, blocking corrupt payloads.
Audit data lineage, pipeline edits, and message publishing metrics in Cloud Logging.
Deploy Dataflow workers inside dedicated service accounts with least privilege roles.
Enforce sensitive data masking inside streaming pipelines using Sensitive Data Protection API.
reliability: Pub/Sub replicates messages across multiple zones in the region to guarantee high availability.
Pub/Sub guarantees at-least-once message delivery; write downstream systems to be idempotent.
Dataflow autoscales worker nodes dynamically based on pipeline backlog (backpressure).
Configure message retention periods in Pub/Sub (up to 7 days) to preserve streams during subscriber outages.
Dead-letter queues isolate failing messages, preventing pipeline crashes.
Enable snapshotting on Pub/Sub subscriptions to replay historical messages for recovery.
Dataplex automatically runs data quality and compliance checks on data lakes.
Dataflow streaming jobs store state in local SSDs, resuming execution during worker failover.
cost: Pub/Sub charges based on volume of data ingested (GB) and message storage duration.
Dataflow bills for compute resource workers (vCPUs/RAM/Disk per hour) used in job execution.
Dataplex billing is based on metadata storage and data processing units consumed.
Use Pub/Sub Lite for predictable, high-volume pipelines to reduce ingestion charges by up to 80%.
Configure Dataflow pipeline autoscaling limits to prevent runaway compute resource billing.
Set appropriate message retention limits; do not store message histories longer than necessary.
Optimize Dataflow worker machine sizes, selecting memory-optimized shapes only when necessary.
Reduce data transfer cost by deploying pipelines, topics, and databases in the same region.

features.name: Declarative Provisioning
features.description: Automates the deployment of GCP infrastructure using declarative configuration files, ensuring repeatability.

features.name: Managed Terraform Engine
features.description: Executes Terraform configurations in a fully managed cloud engine, maintaining state files securely.

features.name: Architectural Blueprints
features.description: Deploys pre-configured, Google-supported blueprints to construct validated enterprise environments.

iamRoles.description: Full access to administer and run managed infrastructure configurations.
Permissions to create projects required during infrastructure runs.

cliExamples.description: Initialize a managed infrastructure configuration run
cliExamples.command:
gcloud beta infra-manager deployments apply my-infra \
  --location=us-central1 \
  --git-source-repo="https://github.com/my-org/gcp-infra" \
  --git-source-directory="terraform/dev"

cliExamples.description: Describe the details and state of a managed deployment
cliExamples.command: gcloud beta infra-manager deployments describe my-infra --location=us-central1

design: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) ensures repeatable, documented, and audited cloud deployments.
Terraform is the industry-standard declarative tool for managing GCP resource lifecycles.
Infrastructure Manager is a managed engine that runs Terraform configurations, managing state files.
Deployment Manager is Google's native declarative template engine using YAML, Python, or Jinja2.
Use Terraform modules to bundle resource configurations (VPC, VMs) into reusable blocks.
Terraform state files must be stored in a remote backend (Cloud Storage) to enable collaboration.
Use state locking (native in Cloud Storage) to prevent parallel executions from corrupting state.
Application Design Center (ADC) drafts visual architecture designs, converting them to IaC scripts.
security: Do not hardcode service account keys or passwords in IaC scripts; pull them from Secret Manager.
Enforce access controls on the Cloud Storage bucket hosting the remote Terraform state file.
Enable object versioning on the state bucket to recover from accidental state corruptions.
Run IaC pipelines using a dedicated Service Account with the least privilege needed to create resources.
Audit every infrastructure deployment, change, and destroy operation in Cloud Audit Logs.
Use policy-as-code tools (like terraform-validator) to scan templates for security violations before runs.
VPC Service Controls protect state storage buckets from unauthorized cross-project access.
Implement branch protection on Git repositories hosting infrastructure code, requiring approvals.
reliability: Remote state files stored in Cloud Storage inherit 11 9s durability and multi-regional replication.
Terraform supports rolling back to a previous infrastructure version by reverting git commits.
Infrastructure Manager handles execution resources, preventing local connection drops from halting runs.
Always execute a 'terraform plan' to verify resource changes before running 'terraform apply'.
Enable logging on state buckets to audit which tools and users are accessing states.
Configure resource lifecycle rules in Terraform (e.g. prevent_destroy) to block accidental deletions of databases.
Store infrastructure code in a Git repository to ensure business continuity during disaster recovery.
Monitor deployment outcomes, errors, and apply statuses using Cloud Monitoring.
cost: Terraform is open-source and free; you pay only for the GCP resources provisioned.
Infrastructure Manager charges based on deployment runs and active state management operations.
Use dry-run options ('terraform plan') to preview resources, avoiding unwanted billing from typos.
Consolidate multiple state files where appropriate to reduce Cloud Storage bucket fees.
Automate the destruction of temporary test environments using cron triggers and pipelines.
Review provisioned resources in state files to identify oversized VM shapes and idle disks.
Use Terraform local-exec commands sparingly to avoid spun-up VM resource overhead.
Analyze cost differences between regional and multi-regional state storage buckets.

features.name: Unified ML Platform
features.description: Manages the complete machine learning lifecycle, from custom model training to endpoint predictions.

features.name: Generative AI Agent Studio
features.description: Visual canvas to design, prototype, and evaluate conversational agents grounded in corporate data.

features.name: Model Garden Repository
features.description: Central repository to browse, test, and deploy Google's Gemini models and open-weight architectures.

iamRoles.description: Full access to administer all Vertex AI datasets, endpoints, models, and pipelines.
Permissions to run models, upload data, and execute training pipelines.

cliExamples.description: List all model deployments in Vertex AI
Get details of an active AI endpoint

design: AI ecosystems are selected to deploy predictive machine learning or autonomous generative agents.
Vertex AI is the core platform, providing unified registries for training, tuning, and prediction pipelines.
Model Garden hosts foundation models (like Gemini 3.5 Pro/Flash) and third-party weights (Llama, Claude).
Agent Development Kit (ADK) builds modular conversational workflows, managing agent tasks.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Engine grounds LLMs with private databases, preventing hallucinations.
Model Armor acts as an inline security firewall, screening prompts and responses for injections.
Colab Enterprise runs managed Jupyter notebook environments with enterprise-grade compliance.
Integrate AI models with Cloud Monitoring to track query latency, prediction drift, and token rates.
security: Model Armor screens inputs/outputs for prompt injections, malware, and sensitive PII leaks.
VPC Service Controls perimeters protect custom training data and models from exfiltration.
Data-at-rest is encrypted by default; configure Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) on storage.
Manage model query access using specific IAM roles (`roles/aiplatform.user`), avoiding admin roles.
Private Google Access is required to allow compute runtimes to trigger AI endpoints privately.
Establish Safety Filters in Vertex AI to block hateful, explicit, or toxic model outputs.
Audit ML training jobs, endpoint query rates, and administrative changes in Cloud Audit Logs.
Restrict client access scopes on VM instances connecting to Vertex AI APIs.
reliability: Vertex AI endpoints scale automatically to meet query spikes, distributing requests across zones.
Foundation models are hosted on Google's globally redundant infrastructure, ensuring high SLAs.
Dataflow pipelines automate continuous data ingestion for retraining machine learning models.
Configure failover endpoints to route traffic to backup models during primary model latency spikes.
Save model training states (checkpoints) to Cloud Storage to resume training after worker failures.
Monitor model prediction drift and accuracy degradation using Vertex AI Model Monitoring.
Client libraries support automatic retries with backoff on model query rate limits (429 errors).
Maintain version controls on model registry artifacts to allow safe rollbacks.
cost: Charged based on custom training compute hours (node/hour), endpoint prediction volume, or token counts.
Gemini models charge per 1K tokens for input and output; Flash is significantly cheaper than Pro.
Model Armor charges a flat rate per million tokens processed after the monthly free tier.
Configure scale-down thresholds (minimum nodes to zero) for custom endpoints to save compute fees.
Use lightweight, cheaper models (Flash) for simple summarization tasks, reserving Pro for complex reasoning.
Clean up old custom model versions, datasets, and inactive Jupyter notebooks to reduce storage fees.
Establish alerts in Cloud Billing to monitor generative AI token consumption.
Use Google's pre-trained APIs (Vision, Speech-to-Text) when possible instead of training custom models.

features.name: API Integration Service
features.description: Connects Google Cloud capabilities directly to your application endpoints via standard API pathways.

features.name: Fully Managed Administration
features.description: Abstracts underlying compute and hosting infrastructures, delivering capabilities as serverless resources.

features.name: GCP Platform Governance
features.description: Integrates with central identity, logging, and security systems to enforce governance.

iamRoles.description: Read-only access to browse resources and view basic metrics.
Permissions to modify configuration states and create application resources.

cliExamples.description: Check configuration settings of the local gcloud SDK CLI client
List all active Google Cloud services whitelisted in the project

design: Platform services provide specialized utility, supporting core compute and storage architectures.
Designed to integrate with other services inside your virtual private cloud (VPC) network.
Access capabilities through standard HTTP REST APIs, gcloud CLI commands, or client libraries.
Requires planning resource naming, project hierarchies, and IAM policies before deployment.
Supports standard JSON configuration schemas, ensuring compatibility across custom workloads.
Exports execution logs and diagnostics metrics directly to the Cloud Logging workspace.
Helps decouple application components, promoting modular microservice designs.
Resources are managed under projects, inheriting parent folder and organization policies.
security: Manage service and resource access using Identity and Access Management (IAM) role permissions.
Protect data transfers by enforcing TLS 1.3 transport-level security for all API endpoints.
Supports placing resources inside VPC Service Controls security perimeters to block data exfiltration.
All data at rest is encrypted by default using Google-managed cryptographic keys.
Assign custom service accounts with least privilege roles to run administrative tasks.
Audit admin actions and data transactions by reviewing logs in Cloud Logging.
Use Secret Manager to secure API credentials, keys, and tokens used by the service.
Disable public resource sharing to comply with corporate security standards.
reliability: Serverless execution models scale resources dynamically to handle unpredictable spikes in load.
Workloads are backed by Google Cloud SLAs, guaranteeing high availability and redundancy.
Data is replicated across zones within the region to prevent loss from hardware failures.
Client libraries support automatic retry logic with backoff to bypass transient network errors.
API rate limiters and project quotas prevent control plane exhaustion.
System modifications, failures, and latency indicators are logged to Cloud Logging.
Replicate configurations to Git repository manifests to enable GitOps-based disaster recovery.
Monitor uptime and endpoint health using Cloud Monitoring uptime checks.
cost: Billed on a pay-as-you-go pricing model based on API transactions, storage, or compute usage.
Free tiers are available for developmental usage limits per month.
Configure console budget alerts in Cloud Billing to monitor spending in real-time.
Prune inactive configurations, idle storage blocks, and orphan assets to avoid leak charges.
Deploy instances in regions with lower resource pricing models to reduce monthly costs.
Limit unnecessary API polling rates by using event-driven architectures (Pub/Sub, Eventarc).
Review cost optimization recommendations in Recommender regularly.
Evaluate committed use discounts (CUDs) for predictable, baseline resource workloads.
